stem: You are the gynaecology registrar in the early pregnancy assessment service reviewing a 29-year-old G2P1 at 8+3 weeks by LMP with 12 hours of light fresh PV bleeding and mild lower abdominal cramping. She is haemodynamically stable. Her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated. She is rhesus negative. Speculum examination shows cervical os closed and minimal fresh blood in the vaginal vault. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a viable intrauterine pregnancy with crown-rump length consistent with 8+2 weeks and foetal heart present at 168 bpm.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender, no peritonism; speculum: cervical os closed, minimal fresh blood; bimanual: uterus 8 weeks size, no adnexal mass or tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124, group and hold sent (Rh negative), beta-hCG 78 000; TVUS: viable IUP, CRL consistent with 8+2 weeks, foetal heart 168, no subchorionic haematoma

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure, advise rest and abstain from intercourse, no anti-D required at this gestation and follow-up with GP
options.rationale: Threatened miscarriage with viable IUP at <12 weeks: reassure (over 90% continue to live birth); anti-D is NOT required for sensitising events under 12 weeks unless the pregnancy is terminated surgically or in ectopic; follow-up with usual antenatal pathway. (Source: RANZCOG Anti-D Guideline; RANZCOG First Trimester Bleeding)

options.text: Administer 250 IU anti-D immunoglobulin intramuscularly for the bleeding event and arrange repeat scan in 7 days
options.rationale: Anti-D is NOT indicated for spontaneous bleeding in viable pregnancy under 12 weeks in Australia; routine administration before 12 weeks is overtreatment. (Source: RANZCOG Anti-D Guideline)

options.text: Commence vaginal progesterone 400 mg twice daily until 16 weeks based on the PRISM trial evidence
options.rationale: PRISM showed progesterone benefit only in women with prior miscarriage AND current bleeding (subgroup); this is her second pregnancy with no prior miscarriage, so progesterone is not first-line. (Source: PRISM trial; RANZCOG)

options.text: Admit for serial beta hCG monitoring with repeat scan in 48 hours to assess for early pregnancy loss progression
options.rationale: Viable IUP confirmed on TVUS does not require serial hCG; serial hCG is for pregnancy of unknown location. (Source: RANZCOG First Trimester Bleeding)

options.text: Surgical evacuation of retained products of conception by suction curettage in theatre within 24 hours arranged
options.rationale: Surgical evacuation is for incomplete or missed miscarriage; this is a viable pregnancy and evacuation would terminate it. (Source: RANZCOG First Trimester Bleeding)

explanation.summary: Threatened miscarriage is defined as PV bleeding before 20 weeks with a viable intrauterine pregnancy and closed cervical os. Over 90% of viable pregnancies with first-trimester bleeding continue to live birth. Investigation is transvaginal ultrasound (foetal heart confirms viability) and beta hCG only if scan is non-diagnostic (PUL). Anti-D immunoglobulin is NOT required in Australia for spontaneous bleeding in viable pregnancy under 12 weeks; it IS required for surgical termination, ectopic pregnancy, or any sensitising event from 13 weeks onwards (250 IU under 20 weeks, 625 IU from 20 weeks). Vaginal progesterone has weak evidence for women with prior miscarriage AND current bleeding (PRISM/ALIFE subgroups), and is not routine. Patient counselling, EPAS follow-up, and clear written safety net (heavy bleeding, severe pain, dizziness, fever) are essential.
key_points: Threatened miscarriage: PV bleed + viable IUP + closed os
>90% continue to live birth if viable on scan
Anti-D NOT required for spontaneous bleed <12 weeks viable IUP
Anti-D required for surgical termination, ectopic, or >=13 weeks
Progesterone benefit only in prior-miscarriage + current bleeding subgroup
explanation.pearls: Australian anti-D dosing: 250 IU for sensitising events from 13 to 20 weeks, 625 IU from 20 weeks onwards including routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks; spontaneous bleed before 12 weeks does NOT require anti-D.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 51-year-old woman with 12 months of disabling hot flushes, night sweats waking her 4 times per night, and new mood lability affecting work performance. Her last menstrual period was 8 months ago. Her BMI is 26. She has no personal or family history of breast cancer, VTE, or cardiovascular disease. Her BP today is 122/74. She still has her uterus and ovaries. She has read about menopausal hormone therapy and wishes to start treatment.

data_table.Examination: No abnormalities of breasts, abdomen, or pelvis on examination
data_table.Investigations: FSH not required for diagnosis at this age with classic symptoms; mammogram up to date; cervical screening up to date

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial menopausal hormone therapy regimen?

options.text: Oral conjugated equine oestrogen 0.625 mg daily as monotherapy with annual review of risk profile arranged
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen in a woman with intact uterus causes endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma; progestogen is mandatory for endometrial protection. (Source: AMS Menopausal Hormone Therapy)

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch 50 mcg twice weekly with continuous oral micronised progesterone 100 mg daily
options.rationale: Continuous combined regimen is for women >12 months amenorrhoeic (postmenopausal); she is perimenopausal with LMP 8 months ago and would need cyclical progesterone to avoid breakthrough bleeding. (Source: AMS Menopausal Hormone Therapy)

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch 50 mcg twice weekly with cyclical oral micronised progesterone 200 mg for 12 days
options.rationale: Perimenopausal women (LMP <12 months ago) need cyclical progesterone to mimic menstrual cycle and avoid breakthrough bleeding; transdermal oestradiol avoids first-pass hepatic VTE risk; micronised progesterone has more favourable breast and VTE profile than synthetic progestins. (Source: AMS Menopausal Hormone Therapy; RANZCOG)

options.text: Tibolone 2.5 mg orally daily as combined synthetic steroid for vasomotor and mood symptoms in this patient
options.rationale: Tibolone is licensed for postmenopausal women (>12 months amenorrhoeic) and is unsuitable for perimenopause; her current pattern needs cyclical bleed-replicating therapy. (Source: AMS Menopausal Hormone Therapy)

options.text: Vaginal oestradiol pessary 10 mcg twice weekly with SSRI venlafaxine 75 mg daily for vasomotor symptoms
options.rationale: Local vaginal oestrogen treats GSM not vasomotor symptoms; SSRI/SNRI is second-line for vasomotor symptoms when HRT contraindicated, not as first-line in absence of contraindication. (Source: AMS Menopausal Hormone Therapy)

explanation.summary: Australian Menopause Society (AMS) recommends menopausal hormone therapy (MHT, formerly HRT) as first-line for moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms in women under 60 or within 10 years of menopause without contraindication (oestrogen-sensitive cancer, undiagnosed PV bleeding, active VTE, severe liver disease, uncontrolled hypertension, recent ACS). Transdermal oestradiol is preferred over oral for VTE risk reduction (avoids hepatic first-pass) and is the choice in overweight, migraine with aura, or VTE risk. Progestogen is mandatory if uterus present: cyclical for perimenopausal (LMP <12 months) to mimic menstrual cycle, continuous for postmenopausal. Micronised progesterone (Prometrium 100 mg daily continuous or 200 mg for 12 days cyclical) has more favourable breast and VTE profile than synthetic progestins. Review symptoms and risks annually. Vaginal oestrogen is for GSM only (vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, urinary symptoms) and has no systemic effects.
key_points: MHT first-line for vasomotor symptoms <60 yo or <10 yr from menopause
Transdermal oestradiol preferred (no first-pass VTE risk)
Progestogen mandatory if uterus: cyclical (perimeno) or continuous (postmeno)
Micronised progesterone preferred over synthetic progestins
Vaginal oestrogen = GSM only, no systemic effects
explanation.pearls: WHI sub-analysis shows transdermal oestradiol carries no excess VTE risk compared with oral oestrogen which doubles VTE risk; this is the basis for preferring patches/gels in overweight women or VTE risk factors.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 31-year-old G1P0 at 11+5 weeks for her booking dating scan. The sonographer identifies a twin pregnancy. Both foetuses are appropriately grown for dates. There is a single placental mass with a thin inter-twin membrane and absence of the lambda sign at the membrane-placenta junction; T-sign is present. Each foetus has its own amniotic cavity. The woman has no significant medical history and conceived spontaneously without fertility treatment.

data_table.Investigations: Dating ultrasound: dichorionic vs monochorionic differentiation - single placental mass, T-sign present at membrane insertion, lambda sign absent, thin inter-twin membrane, 2 separate amniotic cavities, both foetuses CRL consistent with 11+5; routine first-trimester bloods sent

lead_in: What chorionicity does this represent and what surveillance is appropriate?

options.text: Dichorionic-diamniotic twins requiring growth scan and umbilical artery Doppler at 28, 32, and 36 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Lambda sign would be expected in DCDA; T-sign and absent lambda indicate monochorionic placentation requiring more intensive surveillance than DCDA. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Dichorionic-monoamniotic twins requiring growth scan and umbilical artery Doppler 4-weekly from 24 weeks onwards
options.rationale: Dichorionic-monoamniotic does not occur biologically (each chorion develops its own amniotic sac); this is an impossible combination. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Monochorionic-monoamniotic twins requiring inpatient surveillance and elective caesarean at 32 to 34 weeks
options.rationale: MCMA shows absent inter-twin membrane (single amniotic cavity); presence of a thin membrane excludes MCMA. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Monochorionic-diamniotic twins requiring fortnightly growth and TTTS screening ultrasound from 16 weeks
options.rationale: T-sign and absent lambda with 2 amniotic cavities = monochorionic-diamniotic; RANZCOG recommends fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks for twin-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS), TAPS, and growth discordance surveillance with delivery at 36 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Conjoined twins requiring tertiary referral and detailed anomaly scan at 12 to 14 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Conjoined twins are visible on ultrasound as shared body parts; the scan shows 2 separate foetuses in separate amniotic cavities. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Guideline)

explanation.summary: Chorionicity determination in twin pregnancy is most accurate at 10 to 14 weeks and determines surveillance intensity, complication risk, and timing of delivery. Lambda sign (thick triangular projection of placental tissue into the membrane base) indicates dichorionic placentation; T-sign (perpendicular membrane meeting placenta without projection) indicates monochorionic. Dichorionic-diamniotic (DCDA, 70% of twins): two placentas (may appear fused), lambda sign, thick (3-layer) membrane; growth scans 4-weekly from 24 weeks; deliver 37 to 38 weeks. Monochorionic-diamniotic (MCDA, 25% to 30%): single placenta, T-sign, thin (2-layer) membrane; fortnightly scans from 16 weeks for TTTS (Quintero staging), TAPS, sIUGR; deliver 36 to 37 weeks. Monochorionic-monoamniotic (MCMA, <1%): single placenta, no inter-twin membrane, high cord entanglement risk; inpatient surveillance from 24 to 28 weeks; deliver by caesarean at 32 to 34 weeks. Selective foetoscopic laser is treatment for severe TTTS (Quintero stage II or higher).
key_points: Chorionicity best at 10-14 weeks: lambda (DC) vs T-sign (MC)
DCDA (70%): 4-weekly scans from 24 weeks, deliver 37-38 weeks
MCDA (25-30%): fortnightly from 16 weeks for TTTS, deliver 36-37 weeks
MCMA (<1%): inpatient from 24-28 weeks, caesarean 32-34 weeks
TTTS severe (Quintero II+): foetoscopic laser at tertiary centre
explanation.pearls: Twin-twin transfusion syndrome affects 10 to 15% of monochorionic-diamniotic twins, peaks at 16 to 26 weeks, and untreated severe disease (Quintero stage III or higher) carries mortality of about 90% for both twins.

subtopic_detail: Rhesus negative pregnancy and Kleihauer-guided anti-D
stem: You are the obstetrics registrar called to maternity assessment at 30+2 weeks. A 27-year-old G2P1 rhesus-negative woman has been involved in a low-speed motor vehicle accident as restrained driver 2 hours ago, with seat belt across the gravid abdomen. She has mild lower abdominal tenderness. No PV bleeding. CTG over 4 hours is reassuring with no uterine activity. Routine antenatal anti-D 625 IU was given at 28 weeks. Kleihauer test on maternal blood returns showing 35 mL foetal red blood cells in maternal circulation (well above 6 mL threshold).

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 118/76, HR 92, T 36.8, FHR 142 with normal variability
data_table.Examination: Soft seatbelt bruise across the lower abdomen, uterus non-tender, no PV loss, cervix closed on speculum
data_table.Investigations: FBC: Hb 116, plt 244; group: O-negative; antibody screen negative; Kleihauer: 35 mL foetal RBC; CTG: reassuring 4-hour trace
data_table.Medications: Pregnancy multivitamin; routine anti-D 625 IU at 28 weeks 2 weeks ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding anti-D administration?

options.text: No further anti-D needed because routine 28-week prophylaxis was given 2 weeks ago covering this event
options.rationale: Routine prophylaxis covers feto-maternal haemorrhage of about 6 mL or less; 35 mL exceeds the routine dose capacity and requires additional anti-D. (Source: RANZCOG Anti-D Guideline; NBA Anti-D)

options.text: Repeat Kleihauer test in 48 hours and administer additional anti-D only if foetal cells still detected
options.rationale: Foetal red cells clear from maternal circulation over days; awaiting clearance loses the window for sensitisation prevention which is critical in the first 72 hours. (Source: RANZCOG Anti-D Guideline)

options.text: Administer additional anti-D 625 IU intramuscularly as second dose within the 72 hour preventive window
options.rationale: Dose is Kleihauer-guided: 100 IU per 1 mL foetal RBC; 35 mL requires about 3500 IU additional (much more than 625 IU); single 625 IU is insufficient. (Source: RANZCOG Anti-D Guideline)

options.text: Administer additional anti-D 3500 IU (100 IU per mL foetal RBC) within 72 hours and recheck Kleihauer at 48 hours
options.rationale: Kleihauer-guided dosing: 100 IU anti-D per 1 mL of foetal red cells in maternal circulation; 35 mL requires 3500 IU; given within 72 hours of sensitising event; follow-up Kleihauer at 48 hours confirms clearance. (Source: RANZCOG Anti-D Guideline; NBA)

options.text: Administer IV immunoglobulin 1 g/kg as second line because the Kleihauer is well above the standard threshold
options.rationale: IVIG is reserved for established alloimmunisation with severe foetal anaemia; it is not the preventive agent. (Source: RANZCOG Anti-D Guideline)

explanation.summary: Rhesus negative women without anti-D antibodies receive routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis (RAADP) at 28 weeks (625 IU IM, covers FMH up to about 6 mL) and 34 weeks (625 IU IM), plus 625 IU IM postpartum if neonate is Rh-positive. Sensitising events (PV bleed >=13 weeks, abdominal trauma, external cephalic version, invasive procedures, intrauterine death, antepartum haemorrhage) require additional anti-D within 72 hours, dosed by Kleihauer-Betke test (100 IU per 1 mL foetal RBC). Doses over 1500 IU IM should be given IV. Repeat Kleihauer at 48 hours confirms clearance; if foetal cells remain, repeat anti-D and arrange MCA Doppler for foetal anaemia surveillance. Sensitised women (anti-D titre >=1:16 or rising) require tertiary surveillance with MCA Doppler PSV from 18 weeks for foetal anaemia detection (PSV >1.5 multiples of median triggers cordocentesis and IUT).
key_points: RAADP: 625 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks + postpartum if neonate Rh+
Sensitising event: additional anti-D within 72 hours Kleihauer-guided
Dose: 100 IU per 1 mL foetal RBC; >1500 IU give IV
Repeat Kleihauer at 48 hours to confirm clearance
Sensitised: MCA Doppler PSV from 18 weeks; IUT if PSV >1.5 MoM
explanation.pearls: Standard 625 IU anti-D dose covers approximately 6 mL of foetal red cells (about 12 mL of foetal whole blood); above this, Kleihauer-guided dosing at 100 IU per 1 mL fetal RBC is mandatory.

stem: You are the obstetrics registrar reviewing a 33-year-old G3P0+2 at 18 weeks in the high-risk antenatal clinic. Her obstetric history is significant: 2 previous pregnancy losses at 21 and 23 weeks, both with painless cervical dilation followed by rapid delivery. She had no PV bleeding, no abruption, no infection, and no contractions before either loss. Transvaginal ultrasound today shows a cervical length of 22 mm (below 25 mm threshold) without funnelling. Her cervix is closed on digital examination and she is asymptomatic.

data_table.Examination: Cervix closed on digital examination, no PV bleeding or discharge
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: cervical length 22 mm, no funnelling, foetal anatomy normal; FBC, urine MC&S, MSU sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly from 20 weeks until 36 weeks without cerclage consideration this pregnancy
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone is first-line for incidental short cervix without prior history; with history of 2 painless mid-trimester losses, cerclage is the indicated intervention (history-indicated). (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Birth Prevention)

options.text: Bed rest at home, abstain from intercourse, and weekly cervical length scans from 18 to 24 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Bed rest has no evidence for preterm birth prevention; surveillance alone misses the window for cerclage which works best at 12 to 14 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Birth Prevention)

options.text: Ultrasound-indicated cervical cerclage (McDonald or Shirodkar) this admission given short cervix on TVUS today
options.rationale: Combination of history of >=2 prior mid-trimester losses + TVUS cervical length under 25 mm at <24 weeks is ultrasound-indicated cerclage; insertion before 24 weeks reduces preterm birth before 35 weeks by about 30%. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Birth Prevention; RCOG Cerclage)

options.text: Arabin pessary inserted in clinic with weekly review and removal at 36 weeks or earlier if labour develops
options.rationale: Arabin pessary evidence is mixed and is not first-line in Australian practice when classical history-indicated or ultrasound-indicated cerclage criteria are met. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Birth Prevention)

options.text: 17-hydroxyprogesterone caproate weekly intramuscular injection from 16 to 36 weeks for preterm birth prevention
options.rationale: 17-OHPC was the older standard but the PROLONG trial showed no benefit, and FDA withdrew approval in 2023; not first-line for cervical insufficiency. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Birth Prevention)

explanation.summary: Cervical insufficiency presents as painless cervical dilation and second-trimester pregnancy loss without preceding labour, infection, or bleeding. Cerclage is indicated in three scenarios: history-indicated (>=3 prior mid-trimester losses or preterm births, placed at 12 to 14 weeks); ultrasound-indicated (history of 1 to 2 prior preterm births or losses AND TVUS cervical length <25 mm before 24 weeks); physical examination-indicated rescue cerclage (cervix dilated with visible membranes before 24 weeks, no infection, no labour). McDonald cerclage (transvaginal purse-string at cervicovaginal junction) is the standard technique; Shirodkar is alternative; abdominal cerclage is reserved for prior failed transvaginal cerclage. Vaginal progesterone (200 mg nightly) is first-line for incidental short cervix without prior history. Cerclage is removed at 36 to 37 weeks or earlier if labour begins. Tocolysis is not given prophylactically.
key_points: Cervical insufficiency: painless dilation, mid-trimester loss
History-indicated cerclage: >=3 prior mid-trimester losses, place at 12-14 weeks
Ultrasound-indicated: 1-2 prior + CL <25 mm before 24 weeks
Rescue cerclage: dilated cervix with membranes, no infection or labour
Vaginal progesterone first-line for incidental short cervix, no prior history
explanation.pearls: TVUS cervical length under 15 mm at 16 to 24 weeks in a woman with prior preterm birth carries about 50% risk of recurrent preterm delivery before 32 weeks without intervention, and is a strong driver of cerclage placement.

stem:
You are the obstetric registrar called to assist a 28-year-old G1P0 at 39+5 weeks who has been pushing for 2 hours with epidural analgesia. The foetal head is at +2 station and OA position, mostly visible without parting the labia. CTG shows recurrent late decelerations over the past 20 minutes. The midwife reports good descent with last 3 contractions but slow progress.

The mother is exhausted and finding it difficult to push effectively. Bladder is empty. Foetal weight estimated 3500 g. No previous instrumental delivery or shoulder dystocia.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 124/76, HR 102; FHR 110-130 with recurrent late decelerations, variability reduced
data_table.Examination: Cervix fully dilated, head at +2 station OA, caput +, no moulding, vertex visible without parting labia, intact pelvic floor
data_table.Investigations: CTG abnormal with recurrent late decelerations; bladder empty; consent obtained

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue maternal pushing with coached effort for 30 more minutes and reassess if delivery does not occur
options.rationale: Pathological CTG with recurrent late decelerations mandates expedited delivery; continued pushing is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG Operative Vaginal Birth)

options.text: Vacuum-assisted vaginal delivery using a Kiwi OmniCup with maximum 3 cup detachments and 20 minutes total
options.rationale: OA position, head visible without parting labia, +2 station, cooperative pushing maternal effort all favour vacuum; Kiwi OmniCup is the standard Australian device; 3 detachments or 20 minutes are the abandon-criteria. (Source: RANZCOG Operative Vaginal Birth)

options.text: Category 1 caesarean section via emergency theatre transfer with delivery within 30 minutes of decision
options.rationale: At +2 station OA with visible vertex, vacuum is safer and faster than caesarean; caesarean from this point carries higher maternal morbidity and is reserved for failed operative vaginal birth. (Source: RANZCOG Operative Vaginal Birth)

options.text: Wrigley outlet forceps delivery with episiotomy without trial of vacuum or maternal pushing effort change
options.rationale: Forceps are appropriate but not first-choice over vacuum when both are suitable; vacuum has lower maternal trauma though slightly higher foetal scalp injury risk. (Source: RANZCOG Operative Vaginal Birth)

options.text: Augment labour with oxytocin infusion at 1 mU/min and reassess descent after another 30 minutes of pushing
options.rationale: Augmentation in the setting of pathological CTG worsens hypoxia and is unsafe; expedited delivery is required. (Source: RANZCOG Operative Vaginal Birth)

explanation.summary: Operative vaginal birth (vacuum or forceps) is indicated for prolonged second stage, foetal compromise, or maternal exhaustion when cervix is fully dilated, head at +2 station or lower, and position known. Pre-requisites (mnemonic ABCDEFGHIJ): Address patient/Adequate analgesia, Bladder empty, Cervix fully dilated, Determine position, Equipment, Foetal presentation/Adequate maternal pelvis, Gentle traction, Halt at no descent, Incision (episiotomy if needed), Jaw (delivery of head before shoulders). Vacuum (Kiwi OmniCup) is preferred over forceps when both suitable: less maternal trauma but higher foetal scalp injury; abandon if 3 detachments, 20 minutes, no progress with 3 pulls, or 3 contractions. Forceps (Wrigley outlet, Neville-Barnes mid-cavity) used for non-cephalic positions, vacuum failure, foetal distress requiring rapid delivery, or vacuum-contraindicated (prematurity <34 weeks, foetal bleeding disorders). Sequential use of both instruments roughly triples third-stage trauma and is avoided.
key_points: Pre-requisites ABCDEFGHIJ for operative vaginal birth
Vacuum first if both suitable - less maternal trauma
Vacuum abandon: 3 detachments, 20 minutes, no progress
Forceps for vacuum failure, non-cephalic, <34 weeks, foetal bleed risk
Sequential use of both instruments substantially increases trauma
explanation.pearls: Vacuum delivery carries about 10% incidence of cephalohaematoma and a small risk of subgaleal haemorrhage (about 0.06%); subgaleal bleed presents with progressive scalp swelling crossing suture lines and may cause neonatal shock.

stem: You are the obstetric medicine registrar reviewing a 34-year-old G3P2 at day 14 postpartum after an emergency caesarean for placental abruption with intraoperative blood loss of 2800 mL requiring 4 units of red cells and 2 units of FFP. She has been unable to establish breastfeeding despite repeated attempts and has had no menstrual return. Over the past week she reports profound fatigue, cold intolerance, weight loss despite poor appetite, persistent dizziness on standing, and one episode of confusion with measured BSL of 2.4 yesterday morning. She had no postpartum hypertension or eclampsia.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/58 supine, 78/52 standing, HR 76, T 36.1, RR 16
data_table.Examination: Pale, mildly dehydrated, no goitre, no hyperpigmentation, no axillary or pubic hair loss yet, breasts non-engorged with poor expressible colostrum
data_table.Investigations: Na 128, K 4.4, glucose 3.1 (random), Hb 92, TSH 0.4 (low), free T4 7 (low), free T3 3 (low), prolactin 1.8 (very low for postpartum), cortisol 8 am 110 (low), LH and FSH inappropriately low, IGF-1 low

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Initiate oral levothyroxine 100 mcg daily and review thyroid function in 6 weeks at clinic
options.rationale: Starting thyroxine before glucocorticoid in panhypopituitarism precipitates adrenal crisis; cortisol replacement must come first or be given concurrently. (Source: Endocrine Society Hypopituitarism Guideline)

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg now, then 50 mg 6 hourly, with oral hydrocortisone replacement after admission
options.rationale: Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary necrosis after massive PPH) presents with adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism, agalactia, amenorrhoea; secondary adrenal crisis is life-threatening and IV hydrocortisone must be given FIRST, with thyroxine added 24 to 48 hours later. (Source: Endocrine Society Hypopituitarism Guideline)

options.text: Oral fludrocortisone 100 mcg daily and electrolyte replacement with normal saline 1 litre over 4 hours
options.rationale: Sheehan syndrome causes SECONDARY adrenal insufficiency (low ACTH); mineralocorticoid axis is intact (controlled by renin-angiotensin); fludrocortisone is not required. (Source: Endocrine Society Hypopituitarism Guideline)

options.text: Subcutaneous recombinant growth hormone 0.2 mg daily and bone mineral density assessment as outpatient
options.rationale: GH deficiency may co-exist but does not address immediate adrenal crisis risk; replacement priority is glucocorticoid > thyroid > sex steroid > GH. (Source: Endocrine Society Hypopituitarism Guideline)

options.text: MRI pituitary with gadolinium contrast as outpatient in 4 weeks then commence hormone replacement
options.rationale: MRI confirms pituitary infarct/empty sella but delaying treatment risks fatal adrenal crisis; MRI does not change immediate management. (Source: Endocrine Society Hypopituitarism Guideline)

explanation.summary: Sheehan syndrome is postpartum pituitary necrosis from hypovolaemic shock during or after major obstetric haemorrhage; the gravid anterior pituitary is enlarged and uniquely vulnerable to ischaemia. Presentation can be acute (within days, with cardiovascular collapse, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia) or chronic (months to years, with progressive panhypopituitarism). Classic features are failure of lactation (agalactia, prolactin deficiency), failure to resume menses (LH/FSH deficiency), fatigue and weight loss (ACTH/cortisol deficiency), cold intolerance and bradycardia (TSH/thyroid deficiency), and growth hormone deficiency. Replacement priority is glucocorticoid FIRST (IV hydrocortisone then oral 15 to 25 mg daily in divided doses), then thyroxine 24 to 48 hours later (starting thyroxine first precipitates adrenal crisis by accelerating cortisol clearance), then oestrogen-progestogen for women who do not desire pregnancy, with GH and fertility-related FSH/LH as needed. MRI shows empty sella in long-standing disease.
key_points: Sheehan: postpartum pituitary necrosis after massive PPH
Agalactia + amenorrhoea + cortisol deficiency + hypothyroid = classic
Treat glucocorticoid FIRST, then thyroxine 24-48 hours later
Secondary (low ACTH) so no fludrocortisone needed
MRI: empty sella in chronic disease
explanation.pearls: Sheehan syndrome is the commonest cause of hypopituitarism in lower-income countries but is now rare in Australia (incidence about 1 in 100,000 live births); the postpartum failure of lactation, which can otherwise be missed, is the earliest and most specific clinical clue.

stem: You are the maternal foetal medicine registrar reviewing a 36-year-old G4P3 at 32 weeks gestation for tertiary referral. Her obstetric history: 3 previous lower segment caesareans for prior caesarean. Booking ultrasound at 12 weeks raised a query of anterior placenta close to the lower segment. Today's tertiary scan shows anterior placenta covering the internal os, with intraplacental lacunae, loss of the retroplacental clear zone, bridging vessels crossing the bladder-uterus interface, and a focal hyperechoic bulge into the bladder serosa. MRI confirms findings.

data_table.Examination: No PV bleeding, uterus consistent with dates, foetus cephalic
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: anterior placenta praevia + lacunae + loss of clear zone + bridging vessels + bladder serosa bulge; MRI: focal placental invasion through anterior myometrium into bladder serosa; FBC and group/crossmatch valid; Hb 118

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Continue routine antenatal care and plan elective lower segment caesarean section at 39 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Placenta accreta spectrum (here percreta to bladder) requires planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34 to 36 weeks at tertiary centre; routine care at term risks massive haemorrhage and bladder injury. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Spectrum; FIGO)

options.text: Plan elective caesarean hysterectomy at 34 to 36 weeks at tertiary centre with multidisciplinary team
options.rationale: Placenta percreta with bladder involvement requires planned delivery at 34 to 36 weeks at tertiary centre with MDT (MFM, urology, vascular, interventional radiology, anaesthetics, ICU); intentional uterine preservation with placenta left in situ is rarely successful in percreta; caesarean hysterectomy is standard. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Plan elective caesarean section at 37 weeks with attempted manual placental removal and ergometrine if atonic
options.rationale: Attempting to remove an adherent placenta in accreta spectrum causes catastrophic haemorrhage; manual removal is contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Plan vaginal delivery at term with active third stage management and tranexamic acid 1 g prophylactically
options.rationale: Placenta praevia with accreta spectrum mandates caesarean delivery; vaginal birth is contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Conservative uterine preservation with placenta left in situ and methotrexate weekly for 6 weeks post-delivery
options.rationale: Conservative management has high failure rate in percreta (>50% require subsequent hysterectomy and substantial morbidity); methotrexate evidence is poor; reserved for selected cases of focal accreta and fertility preservation. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

explanation.summary: Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) describes abnormal trophoblastic invasion: accreta (attached to myometrium), increta (invades myometrium), percreta (penetrates serosa, often bladder). Risk factors: prior caesarean (most important - relative risk rises with each caesarean from about 3% with one prior to 67% with four or more priors plus praevia), placenta praevia, prior uterine surgery, age >35. Sonographic features at second-trimester morphology and 28 to 32-week tertiary scan: placental lacunae (Swiss cheese), loss of retroplacental clear zone, myometrial thinning <1 mm, bladder line interruption, bridging vessels crossing myometrium, exophytic mass into bladder. MRI confirms depth of invasion. Antenatal diagnosis allows planned delivery at 34 to 36 weeks at tertiary centre with multidisciplinary team (MFM, urology, vascular, IR, anaesthetics, ICU, neonatology, transfusion). Caesarean hysterectomy with placenta left in situ is standard; conservative management with delayed removal and methotrexate is selected for focal accreta where fertility preservation is critical. Maternal mortality is 7 to 10% in unrecognised PAS at delivery.
key_points: PAS spectrum: accreta (myo) < increta (deeper) < percreta (serosa/bladder)
Risk: prior caesarean (each one increases risk) + praevia
Sonographic clues: lacunae, loss of clear zone, bridging vessels, bladder interruption
Delivery: tertiary centre 34-36 weeks with MDT
Caesarean hysterectomy standard; placenta left in situ
explanation.pearls: Women with placenta praevia AND prior caesarean section have a risk of placenta accreta that rises from about 3% with one prior caesarean to more than 60% with four or more priors, which dictates antenatal screening with tertiary ultrasound from 20 to 32 weeks.

stem:
You are the obstetric medicine registrar reviewing a 38-year-old G3P3 at day 6 postpartum after an uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery at term. She presents to ED with progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea (sleeping on 3 pillows), bilateral ankle oedema, and persistent dry cough over the past 4 days. She is breastfeeding. She has no previous cardiac history and had normal ECGs at booking.

She has no chest pain. CXR shows pulmonary congestion with bilateral pleural effusions. ECG shows sinus tachycardia 118 and non-specific T-wave inversion in lateral leads. Echocardiogram by cardiology shows global LV dysfunction with LVEF 28%, mild MR, no thrombus, dilated LV cavity.

data_table.Examination: Raised JVP 7 cm, bilateral basal crepitations to mid-zones, bilateral pitting ankle oedema, no murmur, S3 gallop present
data_table.Investigations: CXR: bilateral pulmonary congestion and pleural effusions; ECG: sinus tachycardia, non-specific lateral T inversion; troponin 0.05 (mildly raised); BNP 1840; echo: LVEF 28% with global hypokinesis, mild MR, dilated LV, no thrombus, no pericardial effusion
data_table.Medications: Iron + folate; paracetamol for perineal soreness

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: IV ramipril 2.5 mg daily plus IV furosemide 40 mg with continued breastfeeding under cardiology review
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in breastfeeding mothers postpartum if breastfeeding continues (captopril and enalapril are exceptions with caution); ramipril is not first-line in breastfeeding. (Source: ESC Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Oral spironolactone 25 mg daily and oral digoxin 125 mcg daily as first-line for postpartum heart failure
options.rationale: Spironolactone and digoxin are second-line or add-on therapies; first-line is loop diuretic + beta blocker + ACE inhibitor (Australian heart failure standard) plus PPCM-specific bromocriptine and anticoagulation. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Heart Failure)

options.text: IV furosemide 40 mg, oral metoprolol 25 mg twice daily, and avoid all anticoagulation due to recent delivery
options.rationale: Postpartum cardiomyopathy with LVEF <35% has high thromboembolic risk; LMWH is indicated unless active bleeding; postpartum status is not a contraindication. (Source: ESC Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: Urgent percutaneous mechanical circulatory support with VA-ECMO at tertiary cardiac centre referral
options.rationale: Mechanical circulatory support is reserved for cardiogenic shock refractory to medical therapy; she is stable on room air with maintained BP and warrants medical therapy first. (Source: ESC Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy)

options.text: IV furosemide, oral bisoprolol, oral enalapril, anticoagulation with enoxaparin, and bromocriptine 2.5 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Peripartum cardiomyopathy is treated with standard heart failure therapy (loop diuretic, beta blocker, ACE inhibitor - enalapril/captopril safe in breastfeeding); LMWH anticoagulation when LVEF <35% (high thromboembolic risk); bromocriptine (prolactin inhibition) is emerging Australian and ESC-supported therapy that may accelerate LV recovery. (Source: ESC Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy; CSANZ)

explanation.summary: Peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) is heart failure with LVEF <45% developing in the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum, in the absence of other identifiable cause. Australian incidence is approximately 1 in 1,500 live births, higher in older mothers, hypertensive disorders, multiple pregnancy, and African ancestry. Pathophysiology involves a prolactin-derived 16 kDa fragment causing endothelial dysfunction, hence bromocriptine (prolactin inhibitor) may accelerate recovery. Management is heart failure GDMT: loop diuretic for congestion; beta blocker (bisoprolol or metoprolol succinate); ACE inhibitor (enalapril or captopril, breastfeeding-compatible) - or ARB if not breastfeeding; aldosterone antagonist; LMWH anticoagulation when LVEF <35% (high thrombotic risk especially around delivery); device therapy (CRT, ICD) considered after 6 months recovery period. About 50% recover to LVEF >50%; relapse risk in subsequent pregnancy is 30 to 50% if recovery incomplete, hence contraception counselling.
key_points: PPCM: LVEF <45% last month of pregnancy or up to 5 months postpartum
GDMT: loop diuretic + beta blocker + ACE inhibitor (enalapril/captopril breastfeeding-OK)
LMWH if LVEF <35%
Bromocriptine 2.5 mg BD adjunctive - accelerates LV recovery (ESC)
Recurrence 30-50% if LVEF not recovered to >50%
explanation.pearls: Bromocriptine 2.5 mg twice daily for 2 weeks then daily for 6 weeks in peripartum cardiomyopathy improved LVEF and reduced mortality in the IPAC and PPCM-bromocriptine trials, and is supported by ESC 2018 guidelines as an adjunct alongside standard heart failure therapy.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 47-year-old man requesting help to quit smoking. He smokes 25 cigarettes per day for the past 30 years, with first cigarette within 5 minutes of waking. He scores 8 on the Fagerstrom Test for Nicotine Dependence (high). He has tried unsupported quit attempts twice with relapse within 2 weeks each time. He has a history of major depressive disorder in remission for 3 years on sertraline 100 mg daily. He has no cardiovascular disease and no renal impairment. He has no history of seizures or eating disorder.

data_table.Examination: Mild nicotine staining on fingers, otherwise unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: FBC, EUC, LFTs all normal at recent annual check; ECG normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy for smoking cessation?

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Varenicline (alpha-4-beta-2 nicotinic partial agonist) is the most effective single-agent smoking cessation pharmacotherapy (about 33% 1-year abstinence vs 18% placebo); RACGP and Quit Victoria recommend it as first-line. Earlier FDA warnings on neuropsychiatric adverse events were removed after EAGLES trial showed no increased risk in stable depression. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation; Quit Victoria)

options.text: Nicotine replacement therapy patch 21 mg daily as monotherapy for 8 weeks followed by gradual reduction
options.rationale: NRT monotherapy is less effective than varenicline or combination NRT (patch + oral) in high-dependence smokers; for FTND 8, combination NRT or varenicline is preferred. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Bupropion sustained release 150 mg daily for 6 days then 150 mg twice daily for 7 to 12 weeks total
options.rationale: Bupropion is acceptable second-line; however the combination of sertraline + bupropion carries a small seizure risk and bupropion is less effective than varenicline in high dependence. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Switch sertraline to fluoxetine and add nicotine gum on demand at urges then taper down over 12 weeks
options.rationale: Switching antidepressants during smoking cessation introduces unnecessary risk of MDD relapse; sertraline does not interact with quitting and should continue. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Refer to specialist mental health team for cognitive behavioural therapy as monotherapy without medication
options.rationale: CBT alone has lower efficacy than CBT plus pharmacotherapy in high-dependence smokers; combination approach is recommended. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation)

explanation.summary: Australian smoking cessation pharmacotherapy is first-line varenicline (alpha-4-beta-2 nicotinic partial agonist), nicotine replacement therapy (combination NRT: patch + oral preferred over patch monotherapy in high dependence), or bupropion. Varenicline is the most effective single agent with about 33% one-year abstinence vs 18% placebo. EAGLES trial (2016) demonstrated no excess neuropsychiatric adverse events in patients with stable mood or anxiety disorders, leading to FDA removal of the boxed warning. Standard varenicline regimen is titration over week 1 then 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks (extend to 24 weeks if successful). Bupropion is avoided in seizure disorder, eating disorder, and concurrent MAOI. Combination NRT (long-acting patch + short-acting gum/lozenge) is preferred over patch alone in highly dependent smokers. Behavioural support (Quitline 13 7848) doubles quit success when combined with pharmacotherapy. Cytisine (Tabex) is an emerging affordable alternative reaching Australian PBS consideration.
key_points: Varenicline: most effective single agent (~33% 1-year abstinence)
EAGLES trial removed mood disorder concerns
Combination NRT (patch + oral) > patch monotherapy in high dependence
Bupropion alternative; avoid in seizure/eating disorder/MAOI
Behavioural support (Quitline) doubles success
explanation.pearls: Varenicline 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks is approximately twice as effective as bupropion 150 mg twice daily and 1.5 times as effective as single-agent NRT for sustained 1-year abstinence; extending to 24 weeks in initial responders further reduces relapse.
